frameworks.name: neuroeconomic value-based decision-making
frameworks.one_line: Willingness-to-pay quantifies in-the-moment economic valuation of drug and nondrug options.

frameworks.name: craving-induced value modulation
frameworks.one_line: Craving multiplicatively amplifies value along a drug-relatedness dimension.

core_question: How do moment-to-moment fluctuations in opioid craving bias the economic valuation of drug-related versus equally desirable nondrug-related options?
key_assumptions: Drug-relatedness is a graded dimension over personalized items
Craving is a separable psychological state from negative mood
manipulations: Time since scheduled opioid maintenance dose (shortly after vs ~24 h later)
Drug-relatedness of consumer items and foods (drug-related vs nondrug-related)
key_findings: Higher trait cravers showed higher drug-related valuation
Within-person, higher momentary craving raised drug-related valuation
Craving increased willingness-to-pay selectively for drug-related items, not for matched nondrug options
The craving-driven value boost grew with offered quantity and was separable from negative mood
interpretations: Craving narrows economic motivation toward the craved object by selectively, multiplicatively amplifying perceived value along a drug-relatedness dimension
